0.text: You could leave life right now. Let that determine what you do and say and think.
0.author: Marcus Aurelius

1.text: The happiness of your life depends upon the quality of your thoughts.
1.author: Marcus Aurelius

2.text: Waste no more time arguing about what a good man should be. Be one.
2.author: Marcus Aurelius

3.text: Very little is needed to make a happy life; it is all within yourself, in your way of thinking.
3.author: Marcus Aurelius

4.text: When you arise in the morning, think of what a precious privilege it is to be alive.
4.author: Marcus Aurelius

5.text: The soul becomes dyed with the color of its thoughts.
5.author: Marcus Aurelius

6.text: It is not death that a man should fear, but he should fear never beginning to live.
6.author: Marcus Aurelius

7.text: Never esteem anything as of advantage to you that will make you break your word or lose your self-respect.
7.author: Marcus Aurelius

8.text: The best revenge is not to be like your enemy.
8.author: Marcus Aurelius

9.text: Accept the things to which fate binds you, and love the people with whom fate brings you together.
9.author: Marcus Aurelius

10.text: How much more grievous are the consequences of anger than the causes of it.
10.author: Marcus Aurelius

11.text: Everything we hear is an opinion, not a fact. Everything we see is a perspective, not the truth.
11.author: Marcus Aurelius

12.text: Dwell on the beauty of life. Watch the stars, and see yourself running with them.
12.author: Marcus Aurelius

13.text: The impediment to action advances action. What stands in the way becomes the way.
13.author: Marcus Aurelius

14.text: You have power over your mind — not outside events. Realize this, and you will find strength.
14.author: Marcus Aurelius

15.text: Our life is what our thoughts make it.
15.author: Marcus Aurelius

16.text: If it is not right do not do it; if it is not true do not say it.
16.author: Marcus Aurelius

17.text: The object of life is not to be on the side of the majority, but to escape finding oneself in the ranks of the insane.
17.author: Marcus Aurelius

18.text: Begin each day by telling yourself: today I shall be meeting with interference, ingratitude, insolence, disloyalty, ill-will, and selfishness.
18.author: Marcus Aurelius

19.text: Loss is nothing else but change, and change is nature's delight.
19.author: Marcus Aurelius

20.text: It is not that we have a short time to live, but that we waste a great deal of it.
20.author: Seneca

21.text: We suffer more often in imagination than in reality.
21.author: Seneca

22.text: Luck is what happens when preparation meets opportunity.
22.author: Seneca

23.text: Difficulties strengthen the mind, as labor does the body.
23.author: Seneca

24.text: Begin at once to live, and count each separate day as a separate life.
24.author: Seneca

25.text: As is a tale, so is life: not how long it is, but how good it is, is what matters.
25.author: Seneca

26.text: No person has the power to have everything they want, but it is in their power not to want what they don't have.
26.author: Seneca

27.text: True happiness is to enjoy the present, without anxious dependence upon the future.
27.author: Seneca

28.text: He who is brave is free.
28.author: Seneca

29.text: If a man knows not to which port he sails, no wind is favorable.
29.author: Seneca

30.text: The whole future lies in uncertainty: live immediately.
30.author: Seneca

31.text: While we wait for life, life passes.
31.author: Seneca

32.text: It is the power of the mind to be unconquerable.
32.author: Seneca

33.text: Sometimes even to live is an act of courage.
33.author: Seneca

34.text: We are more often frightened than hurt; and we suffer more from imagination than from reality.
34.author: Seneca

35.text: The greatest obstacle to living is expectancy, which hangs upon tomorrow and loses today.
35.author: Seneca

36.text: Life is long if you know how to use it.
36.author: Seneca

37.text: Hang on to your youthful enthusiasms — you'll be able to use them better when you're older.
37.author: Seneca

38.text: Every new beginning comes from some other beginning's end.
38.author: Seneca

39.text: Associate with people who are likely to improve you.
39.author: Seneca

40.text: It's not what happens to you, but how you react to it that matters.
40.author: Epictetus

41.text: First say to yourself what you would be; and then do what you have to do.
41.author: Epictetus

42.text: Man is not worried by real problems so much as by his imagined anxieties about real problems.
42.author: Epictetus

43.text: No great thing is created suddenly.
43.author: Epictetus

44.text: Make the best use of what is in your power, and take the rest as it happens.
44.author: Epictetus

45.text: Wealth consists not in having great possessions, but in having few wants.
45.author: Epictetus

46.text: He who laughs at himself never runs out of things to laugh at.
46.author: Epictetus

47.text: Don't explain your philosophy. Embody it.
47.author: Epictetus

48.text: Freedom is the only worthy goal in life. It is won by disregarding things that lie beyond our control.
48.author: Epictetus

50.text: Circumstances don't make the man, they only reveal him to himself.
50.author: Epictetus

51.text: If you want to improve, be content to be thought foolish and stupid.
51.author: Epictetus

52.text: Other people's views and troubles can be contagious. Don't sabotage yourself by unwittingly adopting negative, unproductive attitudes.
52.author: Epictetus

53.text: Know, first, who you are, and then adorn yourself accordingly.
53.author: Epictetus

54.text: Seek not the good in external things; seek it in yourselves.
54.author: Epictetus

55.text: The key is to keep company only with people who uplift you, whose presence calls forth your best.
55.author: Epictetus

56.text: He is a wise man who does not grieve for the things which he has not, but rejoices for those which he has.
56.author: Epictetus

57.text: Any person capable of angering you becomes your master.
57.author: Epictetus

58.text: Caretake this moment. Immerse yourself in its particulars.
58.author: Epictetus

59.text: We have two ears and one mouth so that we can listen twice as much as we speak.
59.author: Epictetus

60.text: I begin to speak only when I'm certain what I'll say isn't better left unsaid.
60.author: Cato

61.text: An angry man opens his mouth and shuts his eyes.
61.author: Cato

62.text: We have two ears and one mouth, so we should listen more than we say.
62.author: Zeno of Citium

63.text: Man conquers the world by conquering himself.
63.author: Zeno of Citium

64.text: He has the most who is content with the least.
64.author: Diogenes

65.text: If you accomplish something good with hard work, the labor passes quickly, but the good endures.
65.author: Musonius Rufus

66.text: We begin to lose our hesitation to do immoral things when we lose our hesitation to speak of them.
66.author: Musonius Rufus

67.text: The universe itself is god and the universal outpouring of its soul.
67.author: Chrysippus

68.text: Treat your inferiors as you would be treated by your betters.
68.author: Hierocles

69.text: Think of yourself as dead. You have lived your life. Now, take what's left and live it properly.
69.author: Marcus Aurelius

70.text: Do not act as if you had ten thousand years to live.
70.author: Marcus Aurelius

71.text: The art of living is more like wrestling than dancing.
71.author: Marcus Aurelius

72.text: Reject your sense of injury and the injury itself disappears.
72.author: Marcus Aurelius

73.text: Receive without conceit, release without struggle.
73.author: Marcus Aurelius

74.text: Nothing is more honorable than a grateful heart.
74.author: Seneca

75.text: A gem cannot be polished without friction, nor a man perfected without trials.
75.author: Seneca

76.text: Religion is regarded by the common people as true, by the wise as false, and by rulers as useful.
76.author: Seneca

77.text: Throw me to the wolves and I will return leading the pack.
77.author: Seneca

78.text: It does not matter how slowly you go as long as you do not stop.
78.author: Seneca

79.text: You are a little soul carrying about a corpse.
79.author: Epictetus

80.text: People are not disturbed by things, but by the views they take of them.
80.author: Epictetus

81.text: Attach yourself to what is spiritually superior, regardless of what other people think or do.
81.author: Epictetus

82.text: No man is free who is not master of himself.
82.author: Epictetus

83.text: If you wish to be a writer, write.
83.author: Epictetus

84.text: No man ever steps in the same river twice, for it's not the same river and he's not the same man.
84.author: Heraclitus

85.text: The only constant in life is change.
85.author: Heraclitus

86.text: Day by day, what you choose, what you think, and what you do is who you become.
86.author: Heraclitus

89.text: The life given us by nature is short, but the memory of a life well spent is eternal.
89.author: Cicero

90.text: A room without books is like a body without a soul.
90.author: Cicero

91.text: Gratitude is not only the greatest of virtues, but the parent of all the others.
91.author: Cicero

92.text: What we achieve inwardly will change outer reality.
92.author: Plutarch

93.text: The mind is not a vessel to be filled but a fire to be kindled.
93.author: Plutarch

94.text: Memento mori — remember that you will die.
94.author: Stoic Proverb

96.text: The best time to plant a tree was twenty years ago. The second best time is now.
96.author: Ancient Proverb

97.text: Life is long enough, and a sufficiently generous amount has been given to us for the highest achievements if it were all well invested.
97.author: Seneca

98.text: Until we have begun to go without them, we fail to realize how unnecessary many things are.
98.author: Seneca
